1:
The opening five words of Mark's Gospel compress a manifesto. Across the Latin and Eastern fathers, the Reformed expositors, and the grammatical-historical commentators, the title is read as simultaneously a factual superscription and a theological declaration: what follows is Gospel—good news, the royal proclamation of redemption—and its subject is Jesus Christ, identified at once as Son of God.

The Latin fathers press the significance of the title given to Christ. Bede sets it in deliberate contrast with Matthew's "Son of David, Son of Abraham": Matthew opens with the human genealogy so that faith may rise from lesser to greater; Mark opens from the summit, naming Christ "Son of God," so the reader approaches the human narrative already knowing who is present. Hilary sharpens the Sonship: "He is the very and proper Son, by origin, not by adoption; in truth, not in name; by birth, not by creation"—a pointed rebuttal of any adoptionist reading. Jerome and Pseudo-Jerome attend to the word Gospel: it means "good news," belonging properly to the kingdom of God and the remission of sins.

The grammatical-historical commentators examine the phrase "the beginning." Robertson notes it may serve as a heading or title either for the Baptist's ministry or for the whole book; Barnes and Ellicott observe the word Gospel itself is here transitioning from its older oral sense (the proclamation of the kingdom) toward its later written sense (the book containing that proclamation). Lightfoot anchors "the beginning" historically: John's preaching and baptism were "the very gate and entrance into the state and dispensation of the Gospel," opening a new era with a new sacrament of admission and pointing to the Messiah as the beginning of the world to come.

A real, if modest, debate runs through the tradition: does "the beginning of the Gospel" serve as a title for the whole book, or does it refer specifically to John's ministry as the inauguration of the new covenant era? Calvin, Wesley, and the majority of Reformers hear it as the latter—the Law and the Prophets came to an end with John (Luke 16:16; John 1:17); the Gospel dispensation opens with him. Robertson and the grammarians allow either sense. All agree on the substance: Mark begins not with genealogy or prologue but with prophetic fulfillment and a title of unambiguous divine sonship.
2:
Mark's composite citation—weaving Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3 into a single introduction—raises a textual question that the tradition addresses directly. The manuscript tradition is divided between "as it is written in the prophets" and "as it is written in Isaiah the prophet"; both readings are strongly attested, and the voices here do not converge on one side without argument.

The Latin fathers, led by Bede, defend the composite quotation on theological grounds: Mark begins with prophetic testimony precisely to show the reader that what follows is no novelty but the fulfillment of the unified prophetic witness. Jerome notes the first element of the citation comes from Malachi, not Isaiah—"by Malachi the voice of the Holy Spirit resounds to the Father concerning the Son." Pseudo-Jerome reads the combined passage as a single compressed prophecy about repentance, the way of the Lord, and John's austere witness.

Among the Eastern Antiochene commentators, Pseudo-Chrysostom offers the most direct solution: Mark has deliberately compressed two prophecies delivered in different places by two prophets into a single citation, a recognized method of the evangelists. Theophylact adds an interpretation of the title "my angel" (messenger): John is called angel not because he is a heavenly being but because of "his angelic life and lofty reverence," and the phrase "before thy face" indicates the intimate proximity of the forerunner to the king—those who walk nearest to kings are their greatest friends.

The grammatical-historical commentators carefully weigh the manuscript evidence. Robertson argues that naming Isaiah as the chief prophet when combining a catena of OT citations was standard practice; the more difficult reading ("Isaiah") is more likely original, since a scribe would more naturally correct it to "the prophets" after noticing the Malachi quotation. Lightfoot traces the specific phrase "as it is written" through Jewish usage, noting that the convention of chaining prophetic citations under a single authority was common in both rabbinic literature and the New Testament. Clarke and Adam Clarke prefer "Isaiah" on text-critical grounds while acknowledging the composite nature of the citation.
3:
The citation of Isaiah 40:3—"The voice of one crying in the wilderness: Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight"—receives relatively brief treatment in the surviving voices, most of which refer the reader to parallel passages in Matthew and Luke.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Gill, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott, Robertson, Wesley) are unanimous: the wilderness cry of Isaiah, addressed originally to exiles in Babylon with God's highway of deliverance in view, is now applied to John's desert ministry as its New Testament fulfillment. Robertson adds that when the delegation from Jerusalem asked John who he was, he himself reached for this very language (John 1:23)—"he was only a voice, but we can still hear the echo of that voice through the centuries."

Cyril of Jerusalem, the one patristic voice here, connects the wilderness cry to baptism: John's ministry marks "the end of the Old Testament and the beginning of the New," and his baptizing in the wilderness was the first-fruits of a new rite. The verse's brevity in the commentary tradition reflects not its insignificance but its clarity: the application of Isaiah's highway image to John's role as forerunner was, for these commentators, self-interpreting.
4:
John's wilderness ministry is defined by two linked activities: preaching and baptizing. The verse generates the richest theological discussion in Mark 1 outside the opening title, because it compresses a major question about the nature and efficacy of John's baptism.

The Latin fathers are precise and largely agreed. Bede draws the critical distinction: John "preached a baptism of repentance for the remission of sins"—meaning he preached the repentance to which his baptism pointed, and the remission to which repentance would lead through Christ. But John's baptism itself could not actually grant remission: "the remission of sins is only given to us by the baptism of Christ." Pseudo-Jerome reads John's austere garb and diet as a kind of embodied preaching—the camel hair, the girdle, the locusts are signs of penitence and prophetic calling, "the rough marks" that made "the paths straight" for the one coming after. Gregory offers a striking allegory: the locusts in John's diet represent the Jews who "leaped up" in promise and then fell to the ground in unbelief, while the wild honey represents the sweetness of the Gentiles eventually drawn into the kingdom.

The Eastern Antiochene commentators reinforce the sacramental distinction. Theophylact articulates it clearly: "The baptism of John had not remission of sins, but only brought men to penitence. He preached therefore the baptism of repentance, that is, he preached that to which the baptism of penitence led, namely remission of sins, that they who in penitence received Christ, might receive him to the remission of their sins." Pseudo-Chrysostom adds that John's disclaimer ("of whom I am not worthy to loose the latchet of his shoe") rules out any comparison of himself to Christ—lest his baptism be confused with Christ's.

The grammatical-historical commentators bring contextual and linguistic notes. Robertson marks John's coming as an epoch (ἐγένετο, not ἦν)—the historical irruption of a new era. Clarke notes that the very name "John" (Yehochanan, "Yahweh's grace") is appropriate for the forerunner of the God of all grace. Wesley states succinctly: John "preached repentance and baptized as a sign and means of it."
5:
The response to John's preaching is described in sweeping terms: "all the land of Judea and they of Jerusalem" came out to him, were baptized in the Jordan, and confessed their sins. The commentators read this as both a historical fact and a spiritually significant act.

The grammatical-historical commentators are attentive to the grammar. Robertson notes the imperfect tenses (ἐξεπορεύετο, ἐβαπτίζοντο)—they were continuously going out, being continuously baptized—describing not a single event but a sustained movement of people to the Jordan. Clarke, drawing on his knowledge of Jewish proselyte baptism, observes that it was invariable custom to require of anyone seeking immersion a solemn renunciation of idolatry and heathen superstition; John's call to confession thus stands in continuity with this practice but applies it now to Jews themselves, treating the nation as if it were a people needing to return to God. Ellicott notes Mark's clarifying phrase "in the river Jordan"—written for readers unfamiliar with Palestinian geography.

Cyril of Jerusalem, the patristic voice here, sees Jerusalem's eagerness to go out to John as confirmation of the city's prerogative to receive good things first; and the public confession of sins before baptism as the honest display of wounds before the physician applies the remedy. For these commentators the sheer scale of the response—all Jerusalem—is a measure of the spiritual hunger that John's preaching awakened.
6:
Mark's description of John's clothing and diet—camel's hair, leather belt, locusts, and wild honey—is read across the tradition as the costume of a desert prophet and as an implicit identification with Elijah.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the details. Robertson clarifies that the garment was rough cloth woven from camel's hair, not camel skin—a distinction that mattered for questions of ritual impurity and social recognition. Ellicott and Barnes note the deliberate parallel with 2 Kings 1:8, where Elijah is described as "a hairy man with a leather belt"—Mark presents John to readers as the Elijah-figure who was to come (Malachi 4:5). Clarke cross-references Matthew 3:4.

John Lightfoot, whose Talmudic learning makes his comments distinctive here, cites specific passages from the Jewish sages about camel-hair garments, confirming that the dress was historically credible and socially recognizable as the clothing of a man of austere religion. Gill draws the broader cultural point: it was customary for penitents and men of the highest holiness to live in deserts, fare hard, and dress plainly—John embodies that tradition completely. No voice here finds anything allegorical in the diet; the emphasis falls consistently on the historical vividness of Mark's portrait.
7:
John's proclamation of the Coming One reaches its focal point in his declaration of unworthiness: he is not fit even to stoop down and loose the latchet of the sandal of the one who follows him.

The grammatical-historical commentators develop the point with care. Ellicott notes that Mark's version is more physically graphic than Matthew's—Matthew has John unworthy to "carry" the sandal (Matthew 3:11); Mark has him unworthy to "stoop down and unloose" it—the posture of bending low makes the self-abasement more vivid. Robertson explains the cultural background: loosening a master's sandal was the task assigned to the lowest slave; John places himself below even that level. The comparison is not rhetorical humility but a sincere, grounded assessment of the infinite distance between the herald and the Lord he heralds.

Gill notes that John's preaching was essentially Christocentric: every element—the dignity of Christ's person, the excellence of his office, the nature and importance of his work—was aimed at directing attention away from the Baptist and toward the Coming One. Clarke adds that "latchet" (the thong by which sandals were bound) was already obsolete in his day but accurately renders the Greek ἱμάντα.
8:
The contrast between two baptisms—John's with water, Christ's with the Holy Spirit—is one of the Gospel's most theologically loaded antitheses. Every voice here reads it as a statement about the difference between the preparatory and the fulfilling, the sign and the thing signified.

The grammatical-historical commentators note Mark's grammatical form: "with water" is in the locative case (ὕδατι), meaning "in water," whereas Matthew uses the preposition ἐν with both elements. Robertson finds this confirms the immersion-in-water character of John's baptism as the form. Ellicott makes a textual observation unique among the voices: Mark alone omits "and fire" from the formula that Matthew and Luke preserve, possibly because the fire-image would be less intelligible—or more alarming—to his largely Gentile readership.

Clarke provides the strongest theological statement: water baptism "is not to be rested in; it is only an emblem of that which you must receive" from Christ. The Spirit cannot be given by a human minister; he is dispensed by the divine Lord alone. Gill frames the verse as a pastoral warning against treating John's baptism as sufficient for those who have received it. Cyril of Jerusalem ties it to the cosmic picture: John was "filled with the Holy Ghost from his mother's womb" and "sanctified" precisely so he could baptize the Lord—but even this extraordinary man "gave not the Spirit himself" and explicitly pointed beyond his rite to Christ's.
9:
The baptism of Jesus by John is one of the most theologically puzzling moments in the Gospel tradition: the sinless one submits to a baptism of repentance. The voices address both the historical fact and the theological rationale.

The Latin fathers speak with Bede's careful reasoning: Christ was baptized "that by being baptized Himself He might shew His approval of John's baptism, and that, by sanctifying the waters of Jordan through the descent of the dove, He might shew the coming of the Holy Ghost in the laver of believers." His baptism is not for his own sake but for ours—an act of solidarity that transfigures the rite for all who will follow.

The Eastern Antiochene commentators, especially Pseudo-Chrysostom, press the logic from a different angle: Christ was "ordaining a new baptism" and came to John's rite—which stood between the Jewish and the Christian forms—to show by participation that he was not himself in need of remission, and thereby to mark the new baptism he would institute as categorically different. The act signals continuity and supersession simultaneously.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke, Wesley) attend primarily to the Marcan telling. Robertson notes Mark's characteristic εὐθύς ("straightway") and the vivid participle σχιζομένους ("being rent asunder")—stronger than Matthew's language of the heavens simply "opening." Mark presents the vision with physical force: the skies are torn, not politely parted. Matthew Henry summarises the devotional mood: Christ's long obscurity in Nazareth yields at last to his first public appearance, and it is as a baptized man emerging from water with the Spirit descending on him.
10:
Mark alone uses the word σχιζομένους—"being rent asunder" or "split open"—to describe what happened to the heavens at Jesus' baptism. This single word carries the verse's entire interpretive weight for the commentators who treat it.

Ellicott draws the contrast explicitly: Matthew and Luke use language of the heavens "opening" (ἀνεῴχθησαν), which suggests a door being drawn back; Mark's verb implies a violent tearing—the same word used in Mark 15:38 for the temple veil being "rent in two" at the crucifixion. The boldness is characteristic of this Gospel's style. Gill clarifies the subject: it was Jesus—not John—who came up out of the water, and it was Jesus to whom the opened heavens and the descending dove were visible. The moment is private to Christ even as it is theologically universal in its significance.
11:
The heavenly voice seals the baptism with the Father's declaration: "Thou art my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased." Every voice here reads this as a Trinitarian disclosure—the Son ascending from the water, the Spirit descending as a dove, the Father speaking from heaven.

Robertson provides the precise grammatical observation: Mark and Luke report the voice as a direct address to Jesus ("Thou art my beloved Son"), while Matthew has it as a declaration to the bystanders ("This is my Son"). Both are historically compatible—the voice could have been heard differently by different recipients, or the evangelists chose to represent its two dimensions. The echoes of Psalm 2:7 ("Thou art my Son") and Isaiah 42:1 ("in whom my soul delighteth") are present across the schools, though without explicit argument here.

Cyril of Jerusalem draws the doxological conclusion: "The Father was well pleased; unless thou also be well pleased in Him, thou hast not life." The declaration is not merely historical information but a summons to the reader's own allegiance. Barnes notes the reference to Psalm 2:7 as the royal Sonship text; Gill focuses on the voice as the "Bath Kol"—the divine voice from heaven known in Jewish tradition.
12:
The Spirit who descended on Jesus at his baptism immediately drives him into the wilderness. The verb Mark uses—ἐκβάλλει, "drives out" or "casts forth"—is the same word he will repeatedly use for casting out demons. The choice is deliberate and theologically charged.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke, Wesley) all mark the contrast with Matthew's "led up" (ἀνήχθη) and Luke's gentler "was led" (ἤγετο). Robertson calls it "bolder"—the Spirit's impulse is forceful, energetic, not a mild invitation. The word does not imply compulsion against Christ's will but the Spirit's vigorous initiative. Wesley draws the general principle that "extraordinary manifestations of God's favour are wont to be followed by extraordinary temptations"—the wilderness follows the Jordan as night follows day in the experience of God's children.

The Latin fathers supply the necessary clarification about which Spirit is meant. Bede cites Luke's preface ("Jesus being full of the Spirit returned from Jordan") to make the point unmistakable: it was the Holy Spirit who led him, not the evil spirit, and anyone tempted after baptism should recognise the same divine pattern rather than being troubled by it.

Chrysostom sees in the scene a pedagogical arrangement for the benefit of all baptized Christians: Christ did this so that "every baptized person might patiently sustain greater temptations after his baptism, nor be troubled, as if this which happened to him was contrary to his expectation." Pseudo-Jerome adds that after the temptation and the angelic ministry, the pattern points to the restoration of creation: "Then the beasts dwell with us in peace, when the flesh lusts not against the spirit."
13:
Mark's account of the temptation is the most compressed of the synoptic accounts—forty days, Satan, wild beasts, angels—and it adds one detail absent from Matthew and Luke: "he was with the wild beasts."

The grammatical-historical commentators treat this detail carefully. Clarke emphasises that the "most remote, unfrequented, and savage part of the desert" was the setting—the wild animals (panther, boar, hyena, jackal, leopard, as Ellicott lists them) intensified the terror of the scene alongside the demonic adversary. Robertson notes that Mark does not narrate the three specific temptations that Matthew and Luke preserve, apparently because he had a different source or purpose; what he adds is the wild beast detail as a vivid stroke of local colour from Peter's recollections.

Wesley observes simply: Christ "was with the wild beasts, though they had no power to hurt him," and commends Mark's supplementary details as "valuable particulars" that the other evangelists lack.

The most theologically developed reading of the wild-beast detail comes from Lightfoot, who reads the scene as an Adam typology: as Adam before the fall had peaceable dominion over the animals in the garden, so the Second Adam, sinless and unfallen, walked among the beasts of the wilderness in undisturbed authority—a sign of the restoration of creation that his victory over Satan would ultimately accomplish. The angels' ministry that follows (Hebrews 1:14) confirms that the whole cosmic order serves the one who has overcome.
14:
Mark uses John's arrest as his starting gun: from that point, Jesus begins his public Galilean ministry. The verse signals the transition from the preparatory era to the era of fulfillment, and the tradition reads it with rich theological depth.

The Latin fathers interpret the sequence typologically. Bede's formulation has become almost proverbial: "When the Law ceases, the Gospel arises in its steps." John imprisoned represents the Law reaching the end of its public function in Judea; Jesus going into Galilee represents the Gospel now moving into the wider world. Pseudo-Jerome extends this temporally and geographically: John in prison = Law in Judaea; Jesus in Galilee = Gospel expanding; eventually Paul among the Gentiles—each stage superseding the former.

The Eastern Antiochene voices draw a more practical lesson. Pseudo-Chrysostom reads Christ's retreat to Galilee not as flight but as the prudent pattern of one who does not hasten his own passion: he teaches believers not to court persecution but to preserve themselves for their appointed work. Theophylact adds that the retirement also signals the completion of one period: "the time of the Law is complete."

The grammatical-historical commentators focus on historical continuity. Robertson notes that Mark's starting-point for the active ministry follows the pattern of Peter's preaching (visible also in Acts): John's imprisonment is assumed as known; its circumstances will be explained later at Mark 6:17. Ellicott observes that Mark and Matthew agree in omitting the entire period of ministry in Judea and Samaria that John's Gospel records (John 2–4), concentrating on Galilee as the field of Peter's recollection. Origen contributes a reflection on the synonymy of "kingdom of God" and "kingdom of heaven"—essentially the same reality, with differing emphases in the language of the two phrases.
15:
"The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand; repent and believe the Gospel." These four clauses are the programmatic summary of Jesus' entire preaching ministry in Mark, and the commentators treat them as a compressed Gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply the specificity. Clarke finds in "the time is fulfilled" a precise reference to Daniel 9:24–27: the seventy weeks appointed by the angel had run their course; God's prophetic clock had reached its appointed hour. He identifies four distinct elements in the proclamation: (1) the divine time-plan executed; (2) the kingdom at hand; (3) repentance required; (4) trust in the Gospel commanded. Barnes adds that "the time" could refer more broadly to the entire apparatus of OT prophecy and promise reaching its destined consummation. Ellicott notes a phrase distinctive to Mark: "believe the Gospel" (πιστεύετε ἐν τῷ εὐαγγελίῳ)—not simply "repent" (Matthew's version) but repent and trust the good news. He draws the parallel to Paul's "in the fullness of time" (Galatians 4:4; Ephesians 1:10).

Wesley hears the declaration as direct fulfillment of Daniel's prophecy: "The time of my kingdom foretold by Daniel is fully come." Gill catalogues the possible referents of "the time"—the end of the Mosaic dispensation, the fixed divine appointment, the era of the Messiah's advent—without choosing among them, since all converge on the same moment. The united voice of these commentators is that Mark's summary is not merely a preface but the core of everything that follows in the Gospel.
16:
From his programmatic proclamation Jesus moves immediately to recruitment. The call of Simon, Andrew, James, and John by the sea of Galilee is narrated with Mark's characteristic swiftness: they are found working, they are called, and "straightway" they follow.

The Latin fathers draw out the providential choice of fishermen. Bede states the principle clearly: "fishers and unlettered men are sent to preach, that the faith of believers might be thought to lie in the power of God, not in eloquence or in learning." God deliberately bypasses the wise and learned so that no human skill can claim credit for what the Gospel accomplishes. Remigius supplies the most memorable image: "Wonderful indeed is this fishing! For fishes when they are caught, soon after die; when men are caught by the word of preaching, they rather are made alive." The net of the Gospel works the opposite miracle from the fisher's net.

The Eastern Antiochene commentators, led by Theophylact, provide historical context: Peter and Andrew had previously been disciples of John the Baptist; having heard John's testimony to Jesus, they briefly followed Christ; and now, after returning to their trade in the wake of John's imprisonment, they are called again—this time definitively. The call is a return to a commitment they had tasted before.

The grammatical-historical commentators observe that Mark's doubled preposition παρά ("along beside") gives the scene spatial vividness, and that ἀμφιβάλλοντας ("casting a net") describes the characteristic side-to-side motion of the circular cast-net.
17:
The call "Follow me, and I will make you become fishers of men" carries a verb that Mark alone records: γενέσθαι—"to become." Robertson draws out its weight: making fishers of men out of fishermen is not an instant transformation but a slow and long process. Christ does not promise what they already are but what he will make them; the "become" is both a commitment and a programme.

Gill reads the call as a total reorientation of vocation: "leave your worldly employments and become my disciples"—the fishing net is set down not merely for a day but in exchange for a new and permanent calling. Robertson adds that preachers are still being made out of working people "who are willing to leave their business for service for Christ"—the process Jesus began with Simon and Andrew continues in every generation.
18:
"Straightway they forsook their nets and followed him." The immediacy of the response is the verse's entire point, and both voices fix on it.

Gill notes that Peter and Andrew left their nets—"which perhaps were their all"—without hesitation or negotiation. Wesley's single sentence carries the tradition's devotional freight: "Happy they who follow Christ at the first call!" The conjunction of "straightway" (εὐθύς) and "forsook" (ἀφέντες) leaves no room for delay, no transition period, no weighing of alternatives. The nets are dropped; the man is followed. Every subsequent generation that has read this verse has heard in it both the cost and the joy of radical discipleship.
19:
The scene moves immediately to a second pair of brothers—James and John, sons of Zebedee—found in the same stretch of shoreline, mending their nets.

Robertson notes the Marcan detail "a little further" (ὀλίγον)—a small spatial precision that gives the narrative a quality of eyewitness memory, consistent with the Petrine source tradition behind Mark. The participle καταρτίζοντας ("mending" or "preparing") describes the careful work of restoring and readying the nets for the next catch; James and John were diligent in their trade when the summons came. Gill adds that the call came as they were still by the sea, maintaining the spatial continuity of the whole episode.
20:
James and John respond as Peter and Andrew had: immediately. But Mark alone records the hired servants left behind with Zebedee—a detail that sheds light on the scale of the family operation.

Ellicott observes that the hired servants (μισθωτοί) indicate a substantial fishing business: this was not a marginal subsistence livelihood but a profitable enterprise employing paid workers. Robertson adds, from Luke 5:7 and Luke 5:10, that the Zebedee operation was part of a larger co-operative with Simon and Andrew's household. The sons left not poverty but prosperity. Gill notes that the call came "as soon as he saw them"—Christ was actively looking for them, having appointed them "long before" to the service he now formalised.
21:
Jesus enters the synagogue at Capernaum on the Sabbath and teaches. The setting—the Sabbath, the synagogue, the gathered people—provides the backdrop for both the teaching and the miraculous expulsion that follows.

The Latin fathers draw out the significance of Christ's manner of teaching. Bede states the principle: "Jesus as the God and Lord of Moses, himself, by the freedom of his own will, either added those things which appeared wanting in the Law, or altered things as he preached to the people"—the contrast with the Scribes is absolute. The Scribes read and interpreted what was already written; Christ speaks as the author of the Law itself. Theophylact notes the Marcan narrative leaps forward "not in the order of history but in the order of mystery," choosing the first Sabbath miracle to open the account of the Galilean ministry.

The grammatical-historical commentators supply contextual information. Barnes notes that synagogue practice allowed any competent person, invited by the presiding elder, to address the congregation after the Scriptures were read—this is how Jesus taught. Ellicott and Robertson note that Capernaum was now Christ's adopted base (Matthew 4:13), from which these early Galilean episodes radiate.
22:
The crowd's astonishment centres on the contrast between Christ's manner and the Scribes': "He taught them as one that had authority, and not as the scribes."

The grammatical-historical commentators explain what this meant in practice. The Scribes built their authority on a chain of tradition—"Rabbi so-and-so said, citing Rabbi so-and-so"—always appealing to precedent, always delegating to prior authorities. Christ spoke on his own account: "But I say to you." Robertson notes the imperfect ἐξεπλήσσοντο describes a sustained state of astonishment, not a single gasp. The contrast was not doctrinal—he was not contradicting Moses—but authoritative in register: he was the source, not a transmitter.

Calvin, the Reformed voice here, notes that the novelty did not lie in new doctrine but in the spirit of the teaching—the power and freedom of one who spoke as the lord of the word he uttered. Barnes adds that "authority" (ἐξουσία) is a key word in this section of Mark: it appears again when the crowd asks "What is this? A new doctrine with authority?" (v.27)—the healing confirms the teaching, and both flow from the same source.
23:
A man with an unclean spirit appears in the synagogue—in the very place of worship—and cries out. The tradition finds in this juxtaposition a striking irony: the demon recognises what the synagogue assembly does not.

The Latin fathers are struck by the irony. Augustine and Bede note that the unclean spirit confesses Christ's holiness in the very setting where the teachers of the Law have just been compared unfavourably to him—the demon perceives what the scribes and rulers will spend the rest of the Gospel denying. Bede reads the demon's presence in the synagogue as evidence of how deeply the enemy had infiltrated the people of God before Christ arrived to drive him out.

The Eastern Antiochene voices (Pseudo-Chrysostom, Theophylact, Pseudo-Jerome) unpack the cry "What have we to do with thee?" (τί ἡμῖν καὶ σοί). Theophylact reads it as an admission of total opposition: "You and we share nothing in common." Pseudo-Chrysostom notes the plural—"What have we to do with thee?"—suggesting that the demon speaks as the representative of a collective, the whole company of evil spirits that fear Christ's coming.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Clarke, Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes) note that "unclean spirit" (πνεῦμα ἀκάθαρτον) is Mark's characteristic term—appearing first here and recurring throughout the Gospel—and observe that the formula "What have we to do with thee?" echoes OT usage (2 Samuel 16:10), denoting a complete want of common interest or claim.
24:
"I know thee who thou art, the Holy One of God"—the demon's confession is the most theologically precise statement in the chapter, and it is entirely involuntary.

The grammatical-historical commentators press the distinction between knowledge and faith. Gill echoes James: "The devils believe and tremble" (James 2:19)—the demon's knowledge of Christ's identity is accurate but produces no saving response. Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson all note the irony: the unclean spirit who confesses "the Holy One of God" is the least likely confessor of that title, and yet his confession is more precise than anything the scribes or rulers will offer.

Lightfoot contributes the most distinctive reading: he documents Jewish exorcism practice, in which naming the holy figure whose authority was invoked—or naming the demon—was believed to give power over it. The demon here attempts the same technique in reverse, naming Jesus in hope of neutralising him. What it discovers is that the name has no power over the one who holds all power. The demon's formulation "Jesus of Nazareth" is the human identification; "Holy One of God" is the divine recognition. The two together are more than the demon intended to confess.
25:
"And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace and come out of him." The economy of the command is itself the demonstration of authority.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke, Barnes, Gill) all note the same thing: no incantation, no elaborate formula, no appeal to a higher name—just two direct imperatives. Robertson notes that φιμώθητι means literally "be muzzled," a strong silencing command. The contrast with Jewish exorcists (who employed long ritual procedures and required multiple attempts) is implicit in the crowd's astonishment at v.27. Christ's authority operates through speech alone—a word, and the demon obeys. Gill observes that the demon is silenced before it is expelled: unwanted and unclean testimony about Christ is refused even when it is technically accurate.
26:
The demon departs in violence: a convulsion (σπαράξαν, "tearing"), a loud cry, and then silence. The commentators read the resistance as the demon's final assertion of malice within the limits imposed by Christ's command.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke, Barnes) note the vividness of Mark's participles: "the unclean spirit, having convulsed him and cried with a loud voice, came out of him." Robertson observes that Luke adds "having done him no harm"—the violence of departure left no lasting injury. Ellicott traces the physical drama as typical of Mark's style throughout this section. Calvin draws the spiritual lesson: the demon's violent resistance serves paradoxically to demonstrate the completeness of Christ's authority—the harder the enemy fights, the more visible the victory when it comes.

Wesley notes the unity of the act: the demon cried out and came out simultaneously—no negotiation, no partial compliance, no delay once the command was given. The word of Christ accomplished exactly what it commanded, no more and no less.
27:
The crowd's reaction ties the exorcism to the teaching: "What is this? A new doctrine with authority! He commands even the unclean spirits, and they obey him." The authorities they had known—the scribes—could teach but not command; Christ does both from the same source.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke) explain "new doctrine" carefully: it does not mean a doctrine contrary to Moses or the prophets, but one unprecedented in the manner and power with which it is delivered and confirmed. The miracle is read as the credential of the teaching—the same ἐξουσία ("authority") that made the teaching astonishing now manifests itself in the physical expulsion of the demon. Robertson notes the crowd debates among themselves, deliberating: "What is this?"—they cannot yet name what they are seeing, but they cannot deny it.

Calvin notes that "new" points to the novelty of the phenomenon: no one had taught or performed exorcism quite like this. The crowd was not offended—they were seized. And the report that follows (v.28) confirms that their astonishment became the vehicle of the news spreading throughout Galilee.
28:
The fame of the Capernaum synagogue episode spread immediately through the whole surrounding region of Galilee. Mark uses an imperfect verb to suggest not a single announcement but a spreading wave of report.

Robertson notes the imperfect ἐξῆλθεν describes the fame going out continuously—not a single event of spreading news but a sustained radiation outward. Gill marks the geographical scope: "all the region round about Galilee"—a wide area, reaching settlements and villages well beyond Capernaum itself. Clarke adds no further theological note; the verse's function is transitional, bridging the Capernaum synagogue to the next scene. The report of authority—in teaching and over demons—went before Jesus wherever he subsequently went in Galilee.
29:
Leaving the synagogue, Jesus and the four disciples go immediately to Simon and Andrew's house, where they find Simon's mother-in-law laid low with fever. The move from the public to the private is characteristic of Mark's narrative pace.

The Eastern Antiochene voices observe the pattern. Theophylact notes the characteristic "straightway" (εὐθύς)—after teaching and casting out the demon, Christ immediately passes to the house. His mercy allows no rest between one act of grace and the next. Pseudo-Chrysostom draws the practical lesson: the disciples are present and see the healing—they will be the ones who must carry these works to the world.

The Latin fathers see the movement from synagogue to house as the Gospel's pattern: public proclamation is followed by private healing; the congregation receives the word, and the household receives the touch. Pseudo-Jerome reads the two scenes typologically: synagogue = Israel; house = the Gentile church that receives Christ when official religion rejects him—though this reading remains an overlay on the plain narrative.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke) note that all four recently called disciples are present—Peter and Andrew as hosts, James and John as guests. Robertson observes this confirms the arrangement described in Luke 5:7–10, where the two pairs of brothers were business partners.
30: Gill records the one voice on this verse: Simon's mother-in-law was afflicted with "a great fever" (Luke's medical precision) and lay ill before Christ arrived. That the disciples "told him of her" is an act of intercession—they brought her need to the healer's attention, trusting his power and mercy to respond.
31:
Christ took her by the hand and raised her up. The fever left immediately. She then ministered to them—a detail all three synoptic Gospels preserve, and for good reason.

Gill notes the completeness of the healing: there was no convalescence, no lingering weakness. She did not need to rest and recover; she rose and served. The immediate service is the proof of the miracle. Theophylact (from the preceding verses) adds the theological point: when Christ heals, the restoration is total—the body serves again as it was created to serve. The woman's ministry to Jesus and his disciples is thus not incidental but the sign and seal of what had just happened.
32:
As the sun set and the Sabbath ended, the city brought its sick to the door of Simon's house. The accumulated needs of a whole day—held back by the Sabbath restrictions on carrying—are released all at once.

The Eastern Antiochene commentators (Theophylact, Pseudo-Chrysostom) explain the timing: the people were scrupulous about the Sabbath law prohibiting the carrying of burdens, and so they waited until evening—"when the sun did set"—before bringing their sick. Only then could they lawfully transport those who could not walk and bring them to Christ. The scale of the response ("all the city gathered at the door") testifies to the faith—or at least the desperate hope—that had already been kindled by the synagogue episode.

The Latin fathers (Bede, Pseudo-Jerome) note that the setting sun carries an added layer: as one age closes, another opens. The end of the Sabbath day becomes a figure of the end of the Sabbath dispensation, with Christ's healing power flowing freely in the new era.

Robertson and Ellicott (grammatical-historical) note the imperfect tenses: "they were bringing," "they were gathering"—a sustained evening scene rather than a single moment, depicting a long stream of people arriving throughout the hours after sunset.
33:
"And all the city was gathered together at the door." The verse is a summary statement of the scale of the evening's healings.

Robertson takes it as a popular hyperbole—"all the city" representing the mass movement of a community galvanised by news of the morning's miracle. Barnes and Gill both note that it reflects the genuine scale: the report from the synagogue had made the rounds, and those with nowhere else to turn came. Wesley hears in the crowd's gathering an implicit prayer—they came with their sick because they had run out of other options, and in coming they made themselves available to Christ's compassion. The verse sets up the summary of healings and exorcisms in v.34.
34:
Jesus healed many who were sick with various diseases and cast out many demons. The scope is wide; the silence imposed on the demons is precise and deliberate.

The grammatical-historical commentators note that Mark writes "many" (πολλούς) rather than "all"—Robertson and Ellicott observe this apparent moderation is reconciled with Luke's "all" by recognising that "all" referred to all who were brought, and "many" to the variety and number healed. The point is not a limitation of Christ's power but a reflection of the scale of the evening.

On the silencing of demons, the Reformed voices are most explicit. Calvin gives the fullest explanation: Christ refused to accept testimony from unclean spirits because their witness would have contaminated the Gospel. An endorsement from demonic sources would not only fail to help—it would actively harm the credibility of Christ's mission, associating him in public perception with the forces he was there to overcome. Clarke adds the complementary point: impure lips are unfit to herald holy truth—even if the content is accurate, the source disqualifies the proclamation. Robertson and Barnes offer a parallel rationale: the premature disclosure of Christ's identity would have accelerated the political and Messianic misunderstandings that he was carefully managing throughout the Gospel.
35:
Before dawn the next morning, Jesus rises and goes to a solitary place to pray. The sequence—the evening of mass healings, the night, the pre-dawn withdrawal—makes the prayer the hinge of the day.

The Eastern Antiochene voices (Theophylact, Pseudo-Chrysostom) read the withdrawal as a deliberate retreat from the crowd's admiration: having healed many and drawn the whole city, Christ slips away before the praise can settle on him. The lesson for the church is explicit in Pseudo-Chrysostom: pray alone, in secret, away from the audience.

Augustine frames the deepest theological question: Christ is himself God, yet he prays as man—and precisely as the Mediator between God and man. His prayer is not weakness but the model of the humanity he took. Bede observes the contrast with the Scribes: they could teach but did not pray; the one who healed and taught also prayed—and prayed before everyone else was awake.

Robertson supplies the precise temporal expression: πρωῒ ἔννυχα λίαν—"very early in the morning, while it was yet night"—an unusual and vivid phrase. The solitary place (ἔρημος τόπος) deliberately echoes the wilderness of the temptation: Christ returns to the place of encounter with the Father as naturally as he had gone there in the beginning. Ellicott and Wesley add simply that the pattern is set for the disciples: private prayer precedes public ministry.
36:
Simon and those with him searched for Jesus—the verb κατεδίωξεν ("hunted him down") is strong, conveying urgency rather than a leisurely look.

Robertson highlights the compound verb: it suggests an energetic, purposeful seeking—Peter leading the others in a determined search for the one who had slipped away before dawn. Clarke notes the motivation implied: the crowd from the night before was still there, or had returned; the disciples were anxious that Christ was not available to meet the popular expectation. Gill observes the plural "they that were with him"—the four disciples together, but Peter at their head, already functioning as the natural leader of the group.
37:
"All men seek for thee." The disciples' report is delivered as good news—as if universal demand were itself a directive for Christ's next move. The next verse shows they have misread the situation.

Gill reads the disciples' words as a sincere but incomplete assessment: they equated popular acclaim with divine purpose, assuming that because everyone wanted Jesus, he should return to where everyone was. Barnes notes that the report is accurate as a report—people were indeed seeking him—but the disciples' implicit inference (that he should therefore come back) fails to reckon with Christ's own understanding of his mission. The verse functions as a foil for v.38: the disciples see the need; Christ sees the calling.
38:
"Let us go into the next towns, that I may preach there also; for therefore came I forth." The verse is Christ's clearest statement of mission priority in Mark 1: preaching, not healing, is the primary errand; and it is to be done broadly, not in one location.

Robertson explains κωμοπόλεις as "market-towns"—larger than villages but smaller than cities; nearby settlements that formed the natural circuit of a Galilean itinerant teacher. Lightfoot supplies the Talmudic sense of "next towns"—adjacent communities within reasonable reach. The phrase "for therefore came I forth" is deliberately open: Ellicott notes it may refer to Christ's departure from the Father, or his departure from Capernaum that morning, or both—the Fathers typically read both layers simultaneously, finding the eternal mission expressed in the immediate movement.

Wesley and Clarke hear the statement as a declaration of divine appointment: Christ came into the world to preach broadly, not to be the resident healer of one city. Gill presses the priority: the healing is a sign accompanying and confirming the word; the word itself is the primary errand, and it requires constant movement from place to place.
39:
The summary statement closes the first movement of Mark's Gospel: Jesus went through all Galilee, preaching in their synagogues and casting out demons. The two activities together—preaching and exorcism—define his Galilean ministry as Mark conceives it.

Robertson notes the imperfect ἦν κηρύσσων—a periphrastic construction describing the continuous pattern of the ministry, not a single circuit. Barnes and Gill both note the scope: all Galilee, synagogue by synagogue—the mission is systematic and thorough. Clarke observes the pattern: first the word proclaimed, then the demon expelled—teaching and healing run together as complementary expressions of the kingdom's arrival. Ellicott and Robertson note that Mark will unfold this summary in the episodes that follow, each episode illustrating one or both of the characteristic acts.
40:
A leper approaches Christ with a petition that combines bold confidence in Christ's power with complete submission to his will: "If thou wilt, thou canst make me clean."

The Latin fathers dwell on the leper's faith as a model of prayer. Augustine and Bede note that he did not say "if thou prayest to God, thou canst"—he addressed Christ directly as the source of cleansing, which is an implicit confession of his divinity. The twofold petition is also the form of perfect faith: no doubt about capability ("thou canst"), full submission on the matter of will ("if thou wilt"). Bede adds that the leper worshipped before he asked—the posture of prayer precedes the petition.

Chrysostom and Theophylact (Eastern Antiochene) reinforce this: the leper's approach already broke the Mosaic quarantine (Leviticus 13:45 required the leper to cry "Unclean! Unclean!" and keep at a distance). His desperate faith drove him past the legal boundary; he trusted Christ's holiness more than he feared the Law's prohibition.

The grammatical-historical commentators (Robertson, Ellicott, Barnes, Clarke) document the Mosaic law on leprosy and note the social and religious desolation the leper had endured: excluded from community, from worship, from all human contact. Wesley observes that the leper came "beseeching him and kneeling"—all the postures of abasement and need.
41:
Christ's response to the leper's plea involves both word and touch. He says "I will; be thou clean"—and he stretches out his hand and touches the man.

Robertson notes the textual debate at this verse: a minority of manuscripts reads ὀργισθείς ("being angry" or "indignant") where the majority reads σπλαγχνισθείς ("moved with compassion"). Most commentators accept "compassion" as the better reading; the minority "angry" variant may reflect Christ's indignation at the ravages of disease and the misery of sin, or possibly the later copyist's interpretation of the sternness in v.43. Whether the emotion is compassion or a righteous anger at the power of disease, the result is the same: Christ acts decisively.

The touch itself is theologically significant. Gill states the principle: Christ's holiness cannot be diminished by contact with what is defiled—it flows outward and overcomes it. He did not become unclean by touching the leper; the leper became clean by his touch. This reverses the normal operation of the Mosaic purity laws, which transmitted defilement outward. In Christ, holiness is the more powerful property.
42:
"And as soon as he had spoken, immediately the leprosy departed from him, and he was cleansed." The verse is a single sentence of healing, and Gill's voice alone covers it.

The instantaneous and total nature of the cleansing—immediate at the word, complete at the touch—proves the divine power behind the act. The Mosaic law on leprosy had no mechanism for cure; it could only manage the disease through quarantine and declare clean those whom God had already healed. Here Christ does what the Law could not: he removes the leprosy in an instant by his own authority and power.
43:
Christ "straitly charged" the healed man and immediately dismissed him. The Greek ἐμβριμησάμενος—used by Robertson and Ellicott—is a strong word, elsewhere translated as "being deeply moved" or "sternly charging"; it carries a note of urgency or even emotional intensity.

The charge is twofold: tell no one, and go show yourself to the priest. Clarke and Robertson explain the priestly requirement from Leviticus 14:2–32: the healed leper was required to undergo a ceremonial examination, bring offerings, and receive the priest's public declaration of cleanness—a process that would take days and constitute an official, verifiable record of the healing. Christ's insistence on this demonstrates his respect for the Mosaic law and provides the religious establishment with incontrovertible evidence. Gill reads the strong charge as emphatic: Christ did not casually suggest this course of action; he pressed it with urgency, knowing what would likely happen if the man did not comply.
44:
"See thou say nothing to any man; but go thy way, show thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing those things which Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them."

Calvin explains the command to silence as prudential: Christ sought to prevent the kind of tumult and political misreading of his mission that popular excitement about miraculous healings consistently produced. By directing the man first to the priest—the lawful authority in matters of leprosy—Christ also upholds the Mosaic order he had not come to abolish (Matthew 5:17). Robertson notes that "for a testimony unto them" (εἰς μαρτύριον αὐτοῖς) means the priestly declaration would serve as formal evidence for the authorities—a witness they could not subsequently deny.

Wesley reads the purpose clause as the key: the healing was not to remain a private secret indefinitely but to become, after the priestly process, a public and legally certified testimony. The silence was temporary—the testimony permanent.
45:
The man disobeyed. He went out and began to proclaim the event widely—and the consequence was direct: Jesus could no longer enter the cities openly but had to stay in the desert places. Yet even there, people came to him from every quarter.

The grammatical-historical commentators read the irony carefully. Robertson notes the imperfect ἤρχοντο ("they were coming")—even in the wilderness, even outside the cities, the stream of people continued. The man's disobedience hampered the form of Christ's ministry but could not ultimately obstruct it. Ellicott and Clarke observe the detail: Jesus himself had to take the position the leper had formerly occupied—staying outside the towns, in the desert places.

Calvin is measured and instructive: "Christ endured the consequence of the man's enthusiasm without complaint." The episode does not end in reproach but in the quiet irony that the leper's grateful proclamation made Christ an itinerant wanderer—which was, in fact, the plan all along (v.38). Gill notes that people came to Christ "from every quarter" even in the desert—a final confirmation that neither disobedience nor logistical difficulty can contain the kingdom's advance. Wesley draws the practical lesson: even sincere gratitude must submit to Christ's explicit instruction; zeal without obedience is its own kind of disorder.
